text:
# Agriculture pawn in international trade tensions

[Report](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/reports/index.html)

[Agricultural](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/agrarisch/index.html)

08/09/2025 • 6 minutes reading

[Jelmer Schreurs](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/jelmer-schreurs/index.html)

After six months of trade war, a deal was concluded between the US and the EU on July 28. The uncertainty that hung over the market seems to be over. The EU is thus maneuvering its way out of the trade war with the US: a unilateral import tariff of 15 percent will become the standard, including for agricultural products. America previously threatened a 30 percent tariff.

## Download the complete report

Read all the facts and developments in the report 'Agriculture as a pawn in international trade tensions'.

- [Agriculture pawn in international trade tensions (September 8, 2025)

  147 KB](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/landbouw-pion-in-internationale-handelsspanningen.pdf)

During the trade war under Trump I, specific products such as Gouda and Edam cheese were still excluded. Although this was expected for a while, this time no exceptions have been stipulated for the agriculture and food industry.

Based on tariff and price elasticities, ABN AMRO estimates that Dutch exports of agricultural products to the US will fall by up to 35 percent. Fish (-152 million euros), fruit & vegetables (-93 million euros) and dairy (-46 million euros) are particularly affected, see table 1. This is equal to 0.5 percent of total agricultural exports.

The impact on the entire sector therefore appears to be limited, although the consequences for individual companies with a high dependence on exports to the US could be significant. It is mainly European exporters of agricultural products with buyers in the US that are taking the hit. The economic damage of a tariff consists of the costs of the import tariff and a decrease in sales as a result of a higher price. Exporter and importer will negotiate with each other about the price and try to pass the tariff on, backwards or forwards in the chain. According to research by Goldman Sachs – an American bank – 15 percent of the damage caused by the import tariff will be charged to European exporters. American importers also bear 15 percent of the damage while American consumers pay 70 percent. Our research is broader in nature; these percentages may be different for food or agricultural products. European exporters can, in addition to partially absorbing the tariff, partly absorb the American demand drop by selling products elsewhere. The EU is therefore strongly committed to new trade agreements with the Mercosur countries and Mexico.

The effects of the American import tax are also reinforced by the weak American dollar (USD). The dollar has lost a lot of value since Trump took office. Currently the rate stands at 1.17 USD/EUR compared to 1.04 USD/EUR at its inauguration. American importers therefore need more dollars to import the same product into Europe. The Economics Department of ABN AMRO expects the euro rate to rise further between 1.17 and 1.25 USD/EUR in 2026.

## European values as change

In addition to the 15 percent tariff, further agreements on so-called non-tariff barriers such as regulation and quotas were published on August 21 and 28. The EU has expressed its intention to scale down and simplify European legislation so that American agricultural producers have easier access to the European market. In concrete terms, this concerns:

- Expansion of European Tariff Rate Quotas (TRQ) - whereby up to a maximum number of goods no import duty has to be paid - for, among other things, cheese, milk powders, soybean oil, salmon, coalfish and shrimps;
- Abolition of import tariffs and quotas on seeds, dried fruits and vegetables, potatoes and fertilizers;
- Weakening of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) – a mechanism for a CO2 tax at the European border – which means that a surcharge on American fertilizer is off the table for smaller companies as of January 1, 2026;
- Investigate whether simplification of food safety certification for pork and dairy is possible;
- Investigate whether simplification is possible around the anti-deforestation legislation (EUDR) that affects cattle and soy, among other things.

metadata.source_title: Agriculture is a pawn in international trade tensions

text:
While the European Commission (EC) is often proud of the Brussels effect – the effect of global adaptation of European regulations and standards – the EC now appears to be making concessions on its standards for sustainability and food safety in order to limit the financial damage to the economy. However, the EC states that the agreement does not affect sustainability legislation. At the same time, this development fits in with a broader trend in which European sustainability legislation is being simplified and scaled down, the so-called Omnibus legislation.

## Uncertainty does not come out of nowhere

It is still unclear how far the European 'intention' goes and at what pace the relaxations will be introduced. Experience shows that agreements regarding certifications and technical product specifications in particular can take a long time, meaning that agreements for specific products have not yet been finalized months or years after the introduction of a trade deal. The deal will only enter into force if the European Parliament and the European Council agree, which could take several months.

Moreover, it remains to be seen whether there is sufficient support in parliament to finally conclude the deal now that the Socialist & Democrats - the second group in the European parliament - intend to vote against the trade deal. Uncertainty also remains on the other side of the Atlantic. In the US, federal lawsuits are underway into the legitimacy of the import tariffs. Moreover, Trump remains fickle, which means that things can suddenly turn upside down.

## Countermeasures off the table

There are also positive points to report about the trade deal: expansion of the TRQs - in addition to more supply and competition on the European market - ensures lower import tariffs for European importers. Furthermore, European countermeasures have been postponed due to the trade deal. This prevents European importers from having to pay import duties on soy and corn. That is good news for the European animal feed industry and livestock farmers, roughly 30 percent of European soy is imported from the US. However, not all European import tariffs will be scaled down. The existing rates will remain in effect for tomatoes, cucumbers, zucchini, apples and pears. No changes have been announced for poultry and beef either.

## Prices under pressure

The wish of the Americans is to bring the European surplus on the trade balance for goods more into balance. With the proposed measures, both trading blocs appear to be taking a step in that direction: the 15 percent import tariff inhibits European exports of goods to the US and deregulation of EU legislation stimulates American exports to the EU. An increasing supply of American agricultural products on the European market will create downward price pressure. The Dutch farmer will therefore be indirectly affected in the medium term.

It is difficult to determine how strong this effect is because detailed agreements are lacking. As far as we know, the dairy sector in particular can expect additional competition from the US, while the greenhouse vegetable, cattle and poultry sectors remain out of harm's way. The impact on the entire agricultural and horticultural businesses therefore appears to be limited. The fact remains that local matters, such as a shrinking livestock population or a crop failure due to weather extremes, have a greater influence on the price formation of European agricultural products. We also expect the impact on European agricultural exporters to remain manageable given the limited trade volumes and the timeline with which the measures come into effect. Here too, other factors – such as a lower dollar exchange rate – have a greater influence on the trade volume between the US and the EU.

## International trading system under further pressure: China and EU also at odds

Not only the trade relationship between the US and the EU has been disrupted, the relationship with the Chinese is also under tension. The EU and China accuse each other of unfair competition, including state aid and dumping of goods. Over the past year, the trading blocs imposed trade restrictions on each other for specific products, such as electric cars and cognac. The Chinese government will add an import tax - ranging between 15.6 and 62.4 percent - on pork as of September 10 to prevent 'dumping' by European exporters, according to the Chinese government. The European Commission calls the allegations questionable and says the duties are not in line with World Trade Organization (WTO) rules. The EC is investigating options to protect the European meat sector against the levies.

The European pork sector mainly exports pig parts that are hardly consumed in Europe, such as legs, ears or intestines. These products represent roughly 15 percent of the total export value of pork and are mainly sold in Asia, including China. For the Netherlands, this concerns an export value of 520 million euros in 2023. The import tax could have a major impact on the pork sector, because the alternative sales markets are limited to Southeast Asia. Another option is to process the parts into animal feed, although the prices are lower.

## More information

Read all the facts and developments in the report 'Agriculture as a pawn in international trade tensions'.

## More information

Read all the facts and developments in the report 'Agriculture as a pawn in international trade tensions'.

- [Agriculture pawn in international trade tensions (September 8, 2025)

  147 KB](https://assets.abnamro.com/api/public/content/landbouw-pion-in-internationale-handelsspanningen.pdf)

## Read more in the agricultural sector

The agricultural sector in the Netherlands is characterized by high productivity and quality. Many countries and organizations therefore see the Netherlands as an agricultural guide country. With an export of more than EUR 100 billion, Dutch agriculture and horticulture makes a strong contribution to our economy.

[View all articles](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/agrarisch/index.html)
